segments.text: Host: Welcome to the panel. Today we are discussing recent findings in cognitive neuroscience.
Guest A: Our lab found that sleep deprivation of more than 24 hours reduces working memory capacity by approximately 35 percent.
Guest B: We replicated that result and also showed that slow-wave sleep specifically is the critical phase for declarative memory consolidation.
Guest A: The hippocampus plays a central role here. Without sufficient slow-wave sleep, hippocampal-neocortical transfer is impaired.
Guest B: Correct, and we showed that adenosine accumulation is the primary driver of this impairment, not cortisol as previously thought.

fullText: Host: Welcome to the panel. Today we are discussing recent findings in cognitive neuroscience. Guest A: Our lab found that sleep deprivation of more than 24 hours reduces working memory capacity by approximately 35 percent. Guest B: We replicated that result and also showed that slow-wave sleep specifically is the critical phase for declarative memory consolidation. Guest A: The hippocampus plays a central role here. Without sufficient slow-wave sleep, hippocampal-neocortical transfer is impaired. Guest B: Correct, and we showed that adenosine accumulation is the primary driver of this impairment, not cortisol as previously thought.
